utterances: If you've worked in disaster relief, should Myanmar be forced to accept aid by air drop or by gun point?
Tell us your story.
Our number here in Washington is 800-989-8255.
Email us, talk@npr.org, and you can also join the conversation on our blog at npr.org/blogofthenation.
We'll begin with Times managing editor Romesh Ratnesar.
He wrote the article, ""Is it Time to Invade Burma?,"" and joins us now from our bureau in New York.
Nice to have you back on the program today.
Thank you.
And why would force be justified in Burma?
Well, any unilateral action of any kind is a last resort, obviously, in any situation like this.

I think the ideal situation would be for the Burmese government to acknowledge the scope of this disaster and to accept the offers of assistance that many around the world, including the United States, have made.
If they do not accept those offers, and so far, they haven't shown much willingness to do so, I think other steps need to be considered.
And there are other tools, short of a military intervention, that can be tried and should be tried.
But the point is there isn't a whole lot of time to waste.
And if you were going to organize a military intervention of some sort, there's less time to waste.
These things take time.
Right, and, I mean, there are different ways of doing it, obviously.
Foreign Minister Kouchner of France has called for unilateral air drops without the government's permission if they don't grant it.

I think the problem is, to some extent, if you do drop relief supplies and food into these areas, if you don't have people on the ground who can administer the supplies, you can deliver them to the right people who can show people how to use the medicines, that there's great potential for misuse, for abuse, for hoarding.
So these things do require a certain amount of logistical preparation and would require some time.
But the United States and other militaries around the world have experience in this, most recently in and most effectively in 2004 in responding to the tsunami.
So we do know how to do this, and the United States has offered the Burmese government a sort of face-saving way in which they could accept assistance but still remain in control of it.
We haven't yet seen whether they are willing to accept it.
Well, the first American flight arrived in Myanmar today, and aid, we're told, is beginning to trickle in.
But the numbers of aid workers needed to distribute this food and medicine and other supplies, they're not being let in, nor is the United States military, with its considerable assets, being let in.
In part because, well, the regime there feels that you're not - well, this could get out of control.
They're worried about staying in power.

That's right, and that's why many people who know Burma better than I do say that even if the regime makes symbolic gestures and allows, as they did today, the C-130 to come into Burma and deliver some food and supplies, that they aren't prepared to accept the kind of intervention that would be required to really deliver relief to the many people who are at stake.
And so far, we are hearing only one-tenth of those in need are being serviced at the moment, and that's just unacceptable.
Well, it seems to me that, in some ways, you're talking about the next disaster, and not necessarily Myanmar.
You're suggesting that if there is more than one tool available, if there's the threat of some form of forcible humanitarian intervention around, governments might be more responsive from the get-go.
Well, I think we have to begin to consider what options are available in situations like this.

United Nations Security Council Resolution 1674 calls on member governments - says that member governments have the responsibility to protect populations from genocide, war crimes, ethnic cleansing, crimes against humanity.

Now it does not refer to a situation - directly to a situation such as the one we have today where you have a natural disaster, people at risk and a government not supplying its own people with the relief necessary.
There is some speculation and there is some debate whether what the Burmese government is currently doing constitutes a crime against humanity.
And if so, according to the U.N.
Security Council resolution, the world has a responsibility to intervene in some way to protect those at risk.
The world is not noted for rapid and quick mobilization.
He was once a charismatic sports star.
Now he wants to lead Pakistan.
He urges his followers, throw out the old corrupt politicians, criminals.
(SOUNDBITE OF RALLY)

That is true, but again, in 2004, when you had a much bigger disaster on a much wider scale throughout the region after the tsunami, what you saw was the governments in the region immediately accept and ask for help and assistance from other countries around the world.
And within 48 hours you had U.S.
assets, U.S.
military assets, and the assets of countries around the world delivering aid to the affected population.
And as Jan Egeland, who was the U.N.
humanitarian assistance coordinator at that time, has said because of that rapid response, there were no lives lost - that the lives lost during the tsunami were almost entirely the actual event.

But the kinds of things that often happen in these sorts of situations, which are the outbreak of disease, starvation, all those things were averted because there was a very quick response by the world community within 48 hours to that disaster.
Well ,you talked about U.S.
military assets, and in order to rush things to Myanmar, again, the United States is far and away the country best-equipped to do that sort of thing.
And you can think of more than a few places, including Myanmar, that would be suspicious of U.S.
military forces, of the Marines landing on the ground.
That's absolutely correct, and that is one of the things that would have to be taken into account if any operation like this were to be considered.
Ideally, as many people say, the U.S.
would give support to other countries who might be deemed more acceptable to the Burmese government to actually carry out these missions.
But there're lots of things the United States can provide, short of putting its own troops on the ground.
There's logistical support.
There's equipment.
There are helicopters.
And again, the U.S.

has offered the Burmese government the right to sit in on any mission, to have Burmese officials on helicopters, on any ships that are going into the area, that we would go in at day and leave at night.
So there are things that can be done that stop short of a full-fledged landing of thousands of Marines on Burmese soil.
Yet, again, the regime would be suspicious that this is - you're talking about regime change.
You're not talking about humanitarian aid.
I think that would be the perception, and that is the problem, and that's why, I think, the government has been so slow to accept assistance of any kind, and it's not just military assistance.
They're not allowing relief workers.
They're not issuing visas to relief workers.
I think we are faced with a set of bad options here, which - I think the government simply will not countenance the idea of a robust intervention from any outsiders.
And because of that, I think, you know, we could be faced with a really epic disaster, unless something else happens.
We're talking with Romesh Ratnesar, the deputy managing editor for Time Magazine, about his article, ""Is It Time to Invade Burma?"" If you'd like to join us, 800-989-8255.
Email us, talk@npr.org.

And again, it is relatively easy to provide - fly in supplies of - mounting this is some sort of - as you say, there's problems with air-dropping of supplies, but European countries, though they have some military assets, don't have anywhere near the kind of assets the United States does.
The United States is obviously stretched a little thin already, in terms of military assets in use, and therefore, the United States government has shown very little interest in participating.
This is - Secretary of Defense Gates said last week that he could see no way that he would try to deliver supplies of aid without the permission of the Myanmar government.
I think that's true.
I think the administration does not have a great deal of interest in intervening unilaterally without some kind of assurance from the Burmese government that that aid will be welcomed.
Again, I think there is a point past which responsible governments around the world have to say this is a crisis in which millions of people are at risk and something can be done.
These things are not unprecedented.
We actually know how to respond.
We know how to deliver aid in situations like this.
And if we can do something, what is stopping us from doing it?

I think this is a debate that is going to be taking place and is already taking place, not just in Washington but at the U.N., European capitals, and again, French Foreign Minister Kouchner has been out front on this.
But I think more people are going to join the chorus, if you don't see more action and more movement from the Burmese government.

And we have just a couple of seconds before the break, but if this is, as you suggest it might be, a ""war crime"" of a sort, at that point, would it be justified to intervene and take the Myanmar military out of the way if it objects?

Well, I think it's highly complicated, and you have to think about what the consequences would be regionally, diplomatically, if you were to take any kind of hostile action against the Burmese military.
What we're talking about here is not necessarily a hostile intervention.
What the United States has offered is a purely humanitarian assistance, delivered by our military with the cooperation of the Burmese government.
It does not have to be something that leads to regime change.
Romesh Ratnesar, stay with us, if you would.
We're going to talk more about the thousands dead after the cyclone in Myanmar and many, many more at risk, and what to do with a reluctant government that doesn't want to accept outside help.
It's Talk of the Nation from NPR News.
(Soundbite of music)
This is Talk of the Nation.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.
We've been talking about the trickle of outside humanitarian aid going to Myanmar after a deadly cyclone killed thousands and stranded many more.
In a moment, we'll speak with a spokesperson from the World Food Programme.
If you've ever worked in disaster relief, we want to hear from you.
Should Myanmar be forced to accept aid by air drop?
Give us a call, 800-989-8255.
Our email address is talk@npr.org, and you can read what other listeners have to say on our blog, at npr.org/blogofthenation.
Joining us now from our office in New York is Bettina Luescher.
She's chief spokesperson for the World Food Programme, and thanks very much for talking with us today.
Thank you so much, Neal, for having me.
And we've heard a trickle of relief supplies being able to get in.
Is that an accurate portrayal?
(Foreign language spoken).
Yes, it has gotten better in the last few days, but still, compared to what we think we should be doing, we really are at a trickle.
But the good news is we are talking to the governments and those contacts are going well, and we are just hoping that we can do more.
But it's - the people need it urgently, and we just have to, with all the U.N.
organizations, NGOs and the international community, much more to help those hundreds of thousands who need help so desperately.
And it's not just the supplies.
It's the people need it to help distribute those supplies, the logistical structure, that sort of thing.
MS.
LUESCHER: It's everything.
It's people.
It's warehouses, storage tents for our food.
It's generators.
And some of that stuff has been coming in, but much more needs - is needed down there.
For example, we estimate that we should move some 375 tons of food per day to the worst affected areas, and at the moment, we are just doing less than 20 percent of that.
So that's - that's crucially important, that we can do more.
Also, when you look, for example, at the weather situation, the weather is going to get worse.
We are now more than a week after this disaster.
People don't have houses.
They don't have shelter.
They don't have plastic tarps.
They don't have food.
They don't have any sanitation.
It's crucially important that aid gets to them.
And if the government continues to be reluctant to let aid and those people necessary to distribute the aid come into the country, what do you do?
Well, our strategy is just to keep on talking and sending more planes.
You know, we, as an aid organization, stay out of some of the political discussions that have been going on, because for us the number one priority is to get food in and other supplies.
And it has been better.
I mean, there was a problem last week that some of - from two planes, aid supplies were impounded.
That situation has been resolved, so that's good.
But if you think about it, we think that we have less than ten percent of the staff, material, general logistics, that's needed to do this operation.
And it's also - if you compare it to the tsunami operation, there, within a couple of days, we had aid flights coming in every hour.
And I was, for example, in the tsunami reason in Banda Aceh where helicopters took off every couple of minutes with rice and other food supplies to the people.
Again, the good news is that we are in good contact.
We have been on the ground in Myanmar since 1994.
We have well-established communication lines with them.
But you know, our message to the government is help us help you, because the people of Myanmar are really in dire straits now.

The United Nations' secretary general today said that he was immensely frustrated at what he called the ""unacceptably slow"" distribution of aid, and said he'd been unable to get the head general of Burma on the phone and had to send him a letter, which seems, well, ridiculously slow under these circumstances.
Well, I think, you know, we have - from the World Food Programme, we have people on the ground who are talking to counterparts on several levels with the Myanmar government.
And that's our strategy, to just keep on talking, and really making clear that this is not a political operation.
We are humanitarians.
We bring food.
That's all we do, and we do it really well.
We do it in 80 countries around the world.
We do it in many complicated, difficult areas of the world.
We have the logistical knowledge and can pull this off, but we need to have more access.
And again, there are positive signs, so we keep on talking with them, and they've been cooperating, for example, at the airport, with the unloading of our planes.
But as of now, we have - WFP has chartered seven planes that have gone in.
Plus we've put food and other supplies on two others.
And obviously, you know, we would like to do more.
Thanks very much for being with us, and good luck to you.
